items.myth: Tanh eliminates the vanishing gradient problem because it is zero-centered.
items.reality: Zero-centering helps optimization bias but does not prevent saturation. For large |x|, tanh'(x) = 1 − tanh²(x) → 0, so deep saturated tanh networks still suffer vanishing gradients — ReLU was adopted partly for this reason.
items.example: A 20-layer all-tanh MLP with saturated hidden units: gradients multiply many near-zero factors; early layers barely update.

items.myth: Tanh and sigmoid are unrelated activations.
items.reality: tanh(x) = 2σ(2x) − 1 — tanh is an affine rescaling of sigmoid. They share the same S-curve shape but differ in range, center, and peak gradient.
items.example: At x=0: sigmoid(0)=0.5, tanh(0)=0. At x=1: sigmoid(1)≈0.73 maps to tanh(1)≈0.76 via 2×0.88−1.

items.myth: Zero-centered means the average activation is always exactly zero.
items.reality: Zero-centered means f(0)=0 and odd symmetry. Actual batch means depend on the distribution of pre-activations; symmetric inputs yield symmetric outputs centered at zero, but biased inputs produce non-zero means.
items.example: If all pre-activations z are +3, tanh(z) ≈ +0.995 for every unit — mean activation is positive, not zero.

items.reality: ReLU dominates deep feedforward hidden layers, but tanh remains standard in RNN/LSTM hidden and candidate-cell paths where bounded, zero-centered states are intentional.
items.example: PyTorch nn.RNN default nonlinearity is tanh; LSTM cell still uses tanh on the candidate state alongside sigmoid gates.
